problem_summary: Predict each country's gold, silver, bronze, and total medal counts for the 2028 Olympics, evaluate the 'Great Coach' effect on performance, and provide strategic recommendations based on event type and quantity influences.
key_techniques: Handling truncated data via Tobit regression and Hurdle model
Mundlak correction for time-invariant country effects
Dynamic Difference-in-Differences with staggered treatment timing
Inverse Probability Weighting for covariate balance
Double Robust Estimator for treatment effect
Feature engineering of dynamic and constant country characteristics
Event count prediction using SARIMAX and ARIMA with host-country exogenous variables
Particle Swarm Optimization for model parameter tuning
Validation against 2024 data and accuracy reporting by medal-count groups
Sensitivity analysis via error in total medal count estimation
Sport Contribution Score (SCS) for event impact quantification
why_it_won: The paper seamlessly integrates econometric models for zero-inflated, truncated data with causal inference frameworks to isolate the 'Great Coach' effect. Rigorous validation, including separate accuracy measures for high- and low-medal countries and first-time medalists, demonstrates reliability. Comprehensive visualizations and sensitivity analyses reinforce the robustness of findings and actionable insights.
section_highlights: Clear problem restatement and flowchart overview guide the reader. Model construction is logically justified (Tobit for truncation, Mundlak for unobserved heterogeneity, Hurdle for zero barrier) with explicit formulas. Results are presented with comparative tables, heatmaps, and line graphs that effectively communicate predictions, country progress, and coach effects.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Assumes athletes active after 2020 are at peak performance
Cannot capture sudden external shocks (political bans, doping scandals)
Coach effect analysis is limited to selected sports and may suffer from selection bias
Merging historical countries (e.g., Soviet Union to Russia) may introduce inaccuracies
Predicting medals for zero-history countries relies on probability thresholds with limited observable data
reusable_patterns: Combine Tobit and Hurdle models to handle truncated and zero-inflated medal counts simultaneously
Apply Mundlak correction by including time-averaged covariates to control for unobserved heterogeneity
Use dynamic DID with event-study design for staggered interventions, reinforced with IPW and Double Robust estimation
Engineer separate dynamic (time-varying) and constant (time-invariant) country features
Validate model accuracy separately by medal tiers (≥10, <10, first-time) to highlight strengths and weaknesses
Predict future event counts with SARIMAX including host-country exogenous effects
Assess sensitivity by comparing aggregate predicted gold/silver/bronze against direct total medal prediction
Define a Sport Contribution Score (SCS) to quantify sport-specific impact and inform strategic recommendations
Visualize country performance changes over time using heatmaps and bar charts for clear communication
